input.query_abstract: The Internet, as well as many other networks, has a very complex connectivity recently modeled by the class of scale-free networks. This feature, which appears to be very efficient for a communications network, favors at the same time the spreading of computer viruses. We analyze real data from computer virus infections and find the average lifetime and prevalence of viral strains on the Internet. We define a dynamical model for the spreading of infections on scale-free networks, finding the absence of an epidemic threshold and its associated critical behavior. This new epidemiological framework rationalize data of computer viruses and could help in the understanding of other spreading phenomena on communication and social networks.
input.hyp_generic_object: cascading component outages across a high-voltage transmission grid
input.hyp_abstract: Cascading outages are the dominant mechanism behind large-area blackouts: the failure of one element redistributes flow and can trip its neighbours, which trip theirs, and so on. The topology of high-voltage transmission grids is uneven, with a minority of substation buses aggregating a large number of incident lines. We investigate how this dispersion in bus connectivity controls whether a local disturbance dies out or propagates system-wide. Representing each element as either in-service or tripped, with tripping transmitted to connected elements above a per-line stress and restoration returning elements to service, we derive mean-field balance equations for the tripped fraction in each connectivity class. On grids with narrow connectivity the model yields a positive critical stress below which disturbances self-extinguish, consistent with N-1 security intuition. When the distribution of incident-line counts is broad enough that its second moment grows without bound, this critical stress vanishes: arbitrarily mild per-line stress sustains a persistent, self-reigniting outage fraction anchored on the most heavily interconnected buses. Iterating the stratified equations and running quasi-steady cascade simulations on synthetic grids confirms that the steady-state outage fraction grows continuously from zero with loading, eliminating any protective margin. We conclude that connectivity heterogeneity, not average redundancy, sets blackout vulnerability, and that hardening or islanding the few highest-degree buses restores a finite threshold far more efficiently than uniform reinforcement of the network.
input.candidate_title: Improving neural networks by preventing co-adaptation of feature detectors
input.candidate_abstract: When a large feedforward neural network is trained on a small training set, it typically performs poorly on held-out test data. This "overfitting" is greatly reduced by randomly omitting half of the feature detectors on each training case. This prevents complex co-adaptations in which a feature detector is only helpful in the context of several other specific feature detectors. Instead, each neuron learns to detect a feature that is generally helpful for producing the correct answer given the combinatorially large variety of internal contexts in which it must operate. Random "dropout" gives big improvements on many benchmark tasks and sets new records for speech and object recognition.
